coredata.dc:title: Three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets modified by graphitized carbon dots: One-step alkali pyrolysis preparation and supercapacitor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bolin
Yu, Mei
Li, Zesheng
Yu, Changlin
Li, Qingyu
Wang, Hongqiang
coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitor is a new type of energy storage device, which has the advantages of high-power property and extraordinary life cycle. Herein, three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets (3-D ACNs) modified with graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) is proposed as a promising electrode material for the supercapacitor. The 3-D ACNs/GCDs are prepared by one-step pyrolysis strategy with the assistance of alkali (KOH) from an organic molecular precursor of Span 20. The crystal structure, morphology, pore structure of the product is investigated by XRD, SEM/TEM and BET techniques, respectively. The capacitive performances of the electrode are studied by CV and CDC techniques. The 3-D ACNs/GCDs product has a well 3-D porous structure and high specific surface area of 1328 m2 g−1. The capacitive performances of the 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode material are satisfactory, revealing a high specific capacitance (202.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and good rate performance (144.2 F g−1 at 5 A g−1) in three-electrode test system, as well as long cycle stability (93.2% or 87.7% capacitance retention after 2000 or 6000 cycles) in two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3-D nanosheets
Activated carbon
Graphitized carbon
KOH
Supercapacitor

Science and Technology Project of Nantong City

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22078071 , 51774100 , 51762006 and 51864007 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2021A1515010125 , 2020A1515010344 ), Maoming Science and Technology Project ( mmkj2020032 ), Guangxi Key Research and Development Program of Science and Technology (GUIKE AB17195065 and AB17129011 ), Guangxi Technology Base and Talent Subject (GUIKE AD18126001 and GUIKE AD17195084 ), Open project of Guangxi Low-carbon Energy Key Laboratory ( 2020GKLLCEM03 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photograph of disc electrode:(A) large area of the whole sheet, (B) disc electrode; Photograph of “button-like” supercapacitor: (C) disc electrode and other accessories, (D) as-assembled “button-like” supercapacitor.
SEM images of 3-D ACNs/GCDs at different magnifications.
TEM images of 3-D ACNs/GCDs at different magnifications.

XRD patterns of comparative products prepared at different temperature: (A) before washing and (B) after washing; SEM images of (C)3-D ACNs/GCDs at 800 °C (before washing) and (D) direct pyrolysis product without KOH.
Schematic diagram for the formation of 3-D ACNs by K2CO3 template strategy.
SEM images of comparative products prepared at different temperature: (A) 400 °C, (B) 500 °C, (C) 600 °C, (D) 700 °C, (E) 800 °C, (F) 900 °C (Span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.50; after water washing).
XRD patterns of comparative products prepared at different Span 20:KOH mass ratio (Span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.00–1:1.00; temperature = 800 °C; after water washing).

SEM images of comparative products prepared at different Span 20:KOH mass ratio (Span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.00–1:1.00; temperature = 800 °C; after water washing): (A) 1:0.00, (B) 1:0.25, (C) 1:0.50, (D) 1:1.00.
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption analysis of 3-D ACNs/GCDs: (A) adsorption/desorption isotherm, (B) pore volume vs. pore size distribution.

Capacitive performances of 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode in 1 mol L−1 KOH electrolyte under three electrode system: (A) current-density-mode CV curves at 50–1000 mV s−1, (B) specific-capacitance-mode CV curves at 50–1000 mV s−1, (C) CDC curves at 1–5 A g−1, (D) specific capacitance at 1–5 A g−1, (E and F) EIS Nyquist plots.

Capacitive performances of 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode in 1 mol L−1 KOH electrolyte under two electrode system: (A) specific-capacitance-mode CV curves at 50–1000 mV s−1, (B) CDC curves at 1–5 A g−1, (C) specific capacitance at 1–5 A g−1, (D) Ragone plots, (E) EIS Nyquist plots, and (F) cycling stability at 1 A g−1.
Comparison performance parameters of 3-electrode and/or 2-electrode supercapacitors based on different 3-D carbon materials from literatures.

row.entry: 3-D activated carbon nanosheets modified with graphitized carbon dots
3-D activated carbon nanosheets modified with graphitized carbon dots

head.ce:title: Three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets modified by graphitized carbon dots: One-step alkali pyrolysis preparation and supercapacitor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of chemical engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming, 525000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Low Carbon Energy Materials, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Low Carbon Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Low Carbon Energy Materials, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitor is a new type of energy storage device, which has the advantages of high-power property and extraordinary life cycle. Herein, three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets (3-D ACNs) modified with graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) is proposed as a promising electrode material for the supercapacitor. The 3-D ACNs/GCDs are prepared by one-step pyrolysis strategy with the assistance of alkali (KOH) from an organic molecular precursor of Span 20. The crystal structure, morphology, pore structure of the product is investigated by XRD, SEM/TEM and BET techniques, respectively. The capacitive performances of the electrode are studied by CV and CDC techniques. The 3-D ACNs/GCDs product has a well 3-D porous structure and high specific surface area of 1328 m2 g−1. The capacitive performances of the 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode material are satisfactory, revealing a high specific capacitance (202.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and good rate performance (144.2 F g−1 at 5 A g−1) in three-electrode test system, as well as long cycle stability (93.2% or 87.7% capacitance retention after 2000 or 6000 cycles) in two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor.

Three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets modified with samll graphitized carbon dots (3-D ACNs/GCDs) is prepared by one-step KOH pyrolysis preparation for high-performance supercapacitor application.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of energy storage device, supercapacitor has attracted extensive attention because of its long cycle life, high power density, wide temperature range and wide application fields [1–3]. However, compared with secondary batteries such as lithium-ion batteries, the energy density of supercapacitors is too low [4,5]. Therefore, improving the energy density of supercapacitors has become an important topic in this field, and electrode materials play an important role in the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors [6,7]. At present, carbon nanomaterials (especially nanostructured porous carbon with high specific surface area) are still the most promising electrode materials for supercapacitors in practical industrial applications [8–10]. The nanoscale dimension, surface morphology, crystal structure and element composition of carbon nanomaterials are the key parameters to regulate their physicochemical properties and capacitance performances.

Currently, carbon nanomaterials with three-dimensional (3-D) constructions (such as 3-D carbon nanosheets and 3-D porous carbon networks) are considered to be one of very promising multifunctional electrode materials, which have a wide range of applications in many fields such as electrochemical energy storage and conversion [11–13]. Due to their high specific surface area, penetrating network structure and controllable building units, 3-D carbon nanosheets and 3-D porous carbon network materials can provide favorable conditions for the design of high-performance supercapacitors, such as greatly increasing the adsorption and storage of electrolyte, and providing excellent stability and durability of 3-D construction materials in electrochemical applications [14–16]. The template synthesis method is one of high-efficiency preparation methods for obtaining 3-D carbon nanosheets and their porous network constructions, including solid template and gas template methods [17–20]. Most solid-state templates (e.g., SiO2 and CaCO3,) need pickling in the subsequent process of removing the template, which greatly limits their large-scale production and application [21,22]. In addition, although the gas template method (such as bubble template) is convenient, the size of bubbles is difficult to control and 3-D carbon products are often obtained with larger pores (hundreds of micrometers) [23,24]. Due to its simplicity and low cost, the development of water-soluble salt template (e.g., NaCl and K2CO3 [25,26]) for the preparation of 3-D carbon nanosheet materials with controllable nonporous network construction is particularly importance.

Recently, our group prepared a 3-D porous graphene electrode material for supercapacitor from graphene oxide (GO) raw material by using low-cost and water-soluble NaCl as 3-D template [25]. The 3-D carbon product is of a high specific surface area of 485 m2 g−1 and a hierarchical porous structure (high proportion of mesopores and macropores, as well as a small amount of micropores). The electrode material shows a desirable specific capacitance (173.5 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and long cycle life (88% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 8 A g−1) [25]. Moreover, our group also prepared a newly 3-D porous graphene-like electrode material for high-performance supercapacitor from Tween 20 organic molecular precursor by using water-soluble K2CO3 as 3-D template in synchronous KOH activation [26]. This 3-D carbon product shows highly 3-D network structure and hierarchical porous structure (with a very high specific surface area of 2017.3 m2 g−1), which provides a higher specific capacitance of 316.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good cycling stability (with a 92.5% capacitance retention after 2000 cycles at 1 A g−1) [26]. Usually, the self-assembly 3-D porous graphene materials show high conductivity but lower specific surface area and poorer specific capacitance (due to the lack of in-plane nanopores on 2-D sheets) [20], while the 3-D graphene-like KOH-activated carbon materials show higher specific surface area but poorer conductivity and power performance (due to the lack of graphitized structures on 2-D sheets) [27–30]. Therefore, the development of graphited carbon modified 3-D activated carbon nanosheet materials could make up for the shortcomings of both, which is a promising approach to simultaneously obtain high specific surface area (high specific capacitance) and high conductivity (high power characteristics).

In this study, cheap “alkali metal hydroxide (KOH)” is used as activator (the chemical activation of KOH can improve the specific surface area of the material), 3-D structure template (water-soluble carbonate (K2CO3) particles generated from KOH by high-temperature carbonization can be used as 3-D structure template) and graphitization catalyst (alkali metal oxide (K2O) generated by KOH after partial air oxidation can be used as graphitization catalyst) precursor, which fundamentally simplifies the synthesis steps of 3-D carbon nanosheets and reduces the cost of materials. The as-prepared product is of a 3-D nanosheet network construction with graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) on the surface (3-D ACNs/GCDs). The introduction of graphitized carbon dots can effectively improve the conductivity of 3-D activated carbon and improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode.

Preparation of 3-D ACNs/GCDs: Dissolve 10 g Span 20 in 40 mL absolute ethanol, and then mix it with 5 g KOH to form a uniform slurry, then put the slurry into a 100 mL graphite reaction crucible with cover, bury it in a stainless-steel box with petroleum coke powder (100 mesh), and then place it in a muffle furnace for heat treatment at 800 °C for 1 h. After cooling, the product is ultrasonically washed with deionized water, filtered, repeated three times, and then vacuum dried to obtain the final 3-D ACNs/GCDs product (temperature = 800 °C, span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.5).

Preparation of comparative products: In the subsequent synthesis process, we optimized the preparation process and experimental parameters to achieve different comparative products, including different synthesis temperatures and different “Span 20: KOH” mass ratios. The specific parameters are as follows: (1) synthesis temperature = 400 °C, 500 °C, 600 °C, 700 °C, 800 °C and 900 °C; (2) “Span 20: KOH” mass ratio = 1:0.00, 1:0.25, 1:0.50, 1:1.00.

The crystal structure and graphitization degree of 3-D ACNs/GCDs samples were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Japan mechanics Corporation, D/max 2500). The morphology of 3-D ACNs/GCDs samples was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-7001E) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM 2100E). In addition, the specific surface area and pore size distribution of 3-D ACNs/GCDs samples were analyzed by using a nitrogen physical adsorption instrument (Micro for TriStar II Plus 2.02).

Under the electrochemical workstation (Chi 660), the “three electrode system” is used to measure the electrochemical performances by cyclic voltammetry (CV), charge discharge (CDC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. The three-electrode system includes working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode. Reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) is the reference electrode, platinum plate is the counter electrode, and glassy carbon electrode is the working electrode. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 10 mg of sample was dispersed in 0.9 mL of alcohol and 0.1 mL of binder (0.05% Nafion, DuPont) to form a uniform mixed slurry, and then 10 μL mixed slurry (with 0.1 mg sample) coated on glassy carbon electrode (Φ = 5.0 mm), and dry under infrared lamp.

We further prepared the sample into disk electrodes, and assembled a two electrode “button-like” symmetrical supercapacitor (2032#) to measure the electrochemical performance. Preparation of disc electrode and assembly process of “button-like” supercapacitor: mixing the sample with the binder polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) at the mass ratio of 95%:5%, adding an appropriate amount of anhydrous ethanol as dispersant, stirring in a magnetic force for 2 h to complete uniformity and evaporate into slurry material. The resulting slurry is then rolled and form an electrode film in 150 μm thickness, and cut into a disk electrode with a diameter of 1.12 cm (7.5 mg ± 0.2 mg) with a rounded standard die (see Fig. 1 A and B). Finally, a two electrode “button-like” supercapacitor is further assembled with a membrane, two disk electrodes and other accessories (see Fig. 1 C and D).

The crystal structure of as-prepared 3-D ACNs/GCDs (pyrolysis temperature = 800 °C, mass ratio of span 20:KOH = 1:0.5) were tested by XRD and the result is shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen from the figure that the sample has two typical characteristic peaks (C (002) and C (110) diffraction peaks) of carbon material at 24 and 44 °C. The peak height is not strong and the peak width is wide, which belongs to a typical amorphous structure [31]. The XRD pattern of 3-D ACNs/GCDs have obvious upturned tails in the low angle region (5–13 °C), which shows that the sample has a large number of nano-porous structures after KOH activation [32]. In addition, at 26.2 °C, a weak characteristic diffraction peak of graphitized carbon (GC) appears, indicating that there are some graphitized micro-crystalline structures in the sample [33]. The inset in Fig. 2 shows the structural formula and picture of liquid carbon precursor of span 20 molecule (C18H34O6) (CAS No: 1338-39-2). The XRD result showed that the porous activated carbon materials were successfully prepared by the one-pot pyrolysis strategy (in synchronous KOH activation) from molecular-level carbon precursor.

The surface morphology and structure of the material were further characterized by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . From the images in lower magnification (×10,000 and ×20,000), it can be found that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs sample has obvious three-dimensional porous network structure and interrelated nanosheet morphology (see Fig. 3 A and B). The interspaces of these networks vary from 200 nm to 1000 nm, and the carbon nanosheets is also hundreds of nanometers in width and 1 to 2 μm in length. The higher-magnification images (×50,000 and ×100,000) show that these carbon nanosheets have leaf-like branching structure and cuspate morphology (see Fig. 3 C and D). By observing the edge position, it can be found that the thickness of these leaf-like nanosheets is about 20 nm. In particular, there are some nanoparticles (~20 nm) on the surface of the nanosheet, which are preliminarily presumed to be graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) (see Fig. 3 D). Overall, the SEM results confirm the 3-D ACNs/GCDs sample has ultrathin three-dimension nanosheet morphology and unique surface structure with carbon-dot modification.

The TEM images of 3-D ACNs/GCDs sample at different magnifications are further presented in Fig. 4 . Obviously, it can be seen from Fig. 4 A and B that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs is made up of a large number of horizontal or standing carbon nanosheets that are correlated together to form a three-dimensional construction. The semitransparent characteristics suggest that these nanosheets have a thin thickness and porous structure. A higher magnification image shows that the surface of the carbon nanosheet is rough and has many nanoparticle (5–20 nm) structures simultaneously (Fig. 4 C). A magnified high-resolution TEM image of two nanoparticles confirmed that they have clear graphited lattice fringes (0.34 nm in D (002) spacing), indicating that these carbon nanoparticles are typical graphitized carbon dots (GCDs) (see Fig. 4 D). These results from TEM are well consistent with XRD and SEM results, by which the 3-D ACNs with GCDs surface modification is confirmed.

In order to explore the growth mechanism of three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets, we further carried out experiments at different synthesis temperatures (400, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 °C) (Span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.50), and carried out XRD crystal structure analysis for “before washing” and “after washing” samples. Fig. 5 A and B show the XRD patterns of “before washing” and “after washing” samples, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 5 A that K2O (PDF# 65-2992) [34] and K2CO3 (PDF# 87-0730) [35] components appear in the “before washing” sample at a lower temperature of 400 °C. In Fig. 5 B, the position of the C (002) diffraction peak of the 400 °C sample after washing is slightly to the left (about 22°), and there is no C (002) diffraction peak, indicating that the sample is an intermediate product that has not been completely carbonized. With the increase of temperature (500–900 °C), it can be seen from Fig. 5 A that the main component of the sample before washing becomes a single K2CO3, and the K2O characteristic peak gradually disappears. As can be seen from Fig. 5 B, the carbonization degree of the sample continues to increase with the temperature increase. At 500 °C, the C (002) diffraction peak has moved to the right to 23°, indicating that the sample is further carbonized. At 600 °C, the intensity of C (002) diffraction peak suddenly decreases, which may be caused by the complete high-temperature cracking of Span organic structure. When the temperature rises to 700 °C, the intensity of C (002) diffraction peak rises and C (110) diffraction peak appears, indicating that the sample is completely carbonized. It should be noted that the samples at 700–900 °C have obvious upturned tails at low angle, while the sample at 600 °C does not, indicating that the effective KOH activation temperature is above 700 °C. In addition, the GC (002) characteristic peaks at 26.2 °C of the three samples at 700, 800 and 900 °C are gradually clear, indicating that the effective partial graphitization temperature is also above 700 °C. Although the characteristic peak of K2O disappears gradually when the temperature rises above 500 °C, the residual trace components are likely to promote graphitization at the temperature above 700 °C, due to the efficient catalysis of alkali metal oxide [36].

In order to further confirm the contribution of K2CO3 component to the formation of three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets, we further observed the SEM morphology of 800 °C sample before washing treatment (see Fig. 5 C). Obviously, the sample did not find the above 3-D nanosheet structure, but a large number of wrinkled film structures. From the rugose 3-D morphology, it can be judged that the sample should be a composite structure of carbon film coated K2CO3. At the same time, we can also make the following judgment: after the washing treatment of the “K2CO3/carbon film” composite sample, the K2CO3 component is rapidly dissolved and released from the interior of the carbon film, resulting in the rupture of the carbon film into a 3-D nanostructure, and the expected “three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets (3-D ACNs)” product can be obtained. In addition, when KOH is not added, the direct pyrolysis product is a large solid carbon material (see Fig. 5 D), which further shows that K2CO3 component is the key to the formation of three-dimensional nanosheet structure. Fig. 6 shows the schematic diagram of the preparation process of 3-D ACNs by K2CO3 template strategy.

A recent study has shown that alkali metal oxides (such as Li2O) can catalyze the transformation of some carbon containing atmospheres (such as CO) into graphene carbon nanosheets [36]. The synthesis steps are as follows: Li2O powder was placed in a tubular furnace, passed with CO gas, then heat treated at 550 °C, and finally to obtain a honeycomb graphene product with three-dimensional structure after washing process. The results show that Li2O reacts with CO to form Li2CO3 during heat treatment, which plays a good three-dimensional template effect. At the same time, C (graphene) structure is formed under the catalysis of Li2O. The specific reaction process is as follows:
(1)
Li 2
O + 2 CO → C
graphene
+
Li 2
CO 3

On the other hand, the KOH activation reactions of conventional carbon materials are as follows. K2O is a key intermediate product (can be used as graphitization catalyst), and CO is the final gas product (carbon gasification process is the key to pore formation) (see Fig. 6 for details) [37,38].
(2)
4 KOH + C →
K 2
CO 3
+
K 2
O + 2
H 2
(3)
K 2
CO 3
+ 2 C → 2 K + 3 CO
(4)
K 2
O + 2 C → 2 K + CO

To sum up, we infer that the generation of “partially graphitized microcrystalline structure” on the “three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets” (i.e., 3-D ACNs/GCDs products) may be due to the catalytic action of K2O (similar to Li2O), which partially converts the gas product of CO into graphitized nanoparticle structure (i.e., GCDs) and loads it on the activated carbon nanosheets, which can greatly improve the conductivity and structural stability of the activated carbon materials. The possible reaction process for generating GCDs structure is as follows (see Fig. 6 for details):
(5)
K 2
O + 2 CO → C
GCDs
+
K 2
CO 3

In particular, our “three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets” product is realized based on “buried heat treatment” technology [39]. The reaction crucible in this technology contains a certain amount of air (containing suitable O2 and CO2 required for the formation of K2O and K2CO3), and then heat treatment is carried out in a high-temperature “muffle furnace” under the burial protection of “petroleum coke powder” (see the left side in Fig. 6). Compared with the high-temperature “tubular furnace” by expensive inert gas protection, the high-temperature “muffle furnace” route is easier to realize mass production of “three-dimensional activated carbon nanosheets” and reduce the preparation cost.

Next, we explore the morphological changes of product at different synthesis temperatures to further verify the above growth mechanism and evolution process. Fig. 7 is the SEM image of product at different synthesis temperatures (400–900 °C) (Span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.50; after washing). It can be seen from the figure that the sample at 400 °C has a granular structure and belongs to an intermediate product that has not been completely carbonized and cracked (Fig. 7 A). Most of the samples at 500 °C still have granular structure, but the particle size becomes larger (Fig. 7 B). The nanosheet structure of the sample at 600 °C indicates that the cracking reaction occurs (Fig. 7 C), while the nanosheet structure of the sample at 700 °C is more obvious and basically completely carbonized (Fig. 7 D). The 800 °C sample is three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets with good nanosheets network structure (Fig. 7 E), but the 900 °C sample has broken three-dimensional structure, which may be caused by excessive activation temperature (Fig. 7 F).

In order to investigate the structural evolution of three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets, we further carried out experiments at different mass ratios of Span 20:KOH. Fig. 8 shows the XRD patterns of samples prepared under different Span 20:KOH mass ratio of 1:0.00, 1:0.25, 1:0.50 and 1:1.00 (synthesis temperature = 800 °C; after washing). It can be seen from the figure that these samples have two typical carbon material characteristic peaks at 24 and 44°, C (002) and C (110) diffraction peaks. The peak height is not strong and the peak width is wide, which belongs to a typical amorphous structure. These samples prepared with KOH in the low angle region (5–13°) have obvious upturned tails, indicating their nanopore structures (the nanopore structure of the samples with mass ratio of 1:0.50 is particularly prominent). In addition, at 26.2°, with the increase of KOH content, a weak characteristic diffraction peak of graphite carbon (GC) appears, indicating that there are some graphitized products in these samples.

Fig. 9 shows the SEM images of samples prepared under different mass ratios of Span 20:KOH. From Fig. 9 A, it can be seen that the product obtained by direct heat treatment without adding KOH is 1–5 μ m bulk structure. After adding a small amount of KOH, that is, when the Span 20:KOH mass ratio is 1:0.25 (Fig. 9 B), a large number of nanosheets appear in the sample, but the thickness of local nanosheets is thicker (about 100 nm). With the increase of the amount of KOH (mass ratio is 1: 0.50), the sample has a large area of three-dimensional nanosheets, and has a good network structure, and the thickness of the nanosheets becomes very thin (<20 nm). However, as the amount of KOH continues to increase (mass ratio is 1: 1.00), the three-dimensional carbon nanosheets of the sample become fragmented, and the thickness of local nanosheets also increases (>50 nm). To sum up, optimized preparation conditions for 3-D ACNs/GCDs are as follows: Span 20:KOH mass ratio = 1:0.50 at synthesis temperature of 800 °C. Particularly, an appropriate amount of KOH dose can obtain abundant K2CO3 component that acts as the growth template of “three-dimensional structure” for the 3-D activated carbon nanosheets. In many previous literatures on KOH activation, the mass ratio of carbon and KOH (or solid carbon precursor) is usually as high as 1:2–1:5 [32,38]. In this study, our highest mass ratio of Span 20 and KOH is 1:1. This is mainly because span 20 molecule is a liquid carbon precursor, and the carbon content is much lower than those of other solid precursors (such as biomass [14] and polymers [27]). We have tried preparation in 1:2 mass ratio of Span 20:KOH, but unfortunately, almost no activated carbon products can be obtained (because KOH content is too high, carbon product is corroded and gasified). Due to the lower mass ratio of carbon precursor:KOH (1:0.5), the proposed one-step alkali pyrolysis preparation strategy reveals additional advantages, such as lower cost, lower instrument corrosion and easier mass preparation.

Fig. 10 shows the nitrogen adsorption-desorption curves for 3-D ACNs/GCDs sample at the optimized preparation conditions (pyrolysis temperature = 800 °C, mass ratio of span 20:KOH = 1:0.5). From the isotherms in Fig. 10 A, it can be found that the sample has the combined characteristics of type II and type IV isotherms, and has typical characteristics of hierarchical porous (microporous-mesoporous-macroporous) materials [26,40]. In the low-pressure stage, P/P0 = 0–0.02, the curve shows higher point density and higher adsorption volume increase (with a Knee), which indicates that the material has a large number of micropores. In the medium-pressure stage, P/P0 = 0.4–0.8, the curve shows the typical characteristics of mesoporous: there is a narrow desorption Hysteresis loop in the curve, which indicates that the material has an appropriate amount of mesopores. In addition, in the high-pressure stage, P/P0 = 0.8–1.0, the obvious increase of adsorption volume occurs again (with an inverted L type), indicating that some macroporous structures exist in the sample. After calculation, the BET and Langmuir specific surface of the sample is as high as 1328 m2 g−1 and 1445 m2 g−1, respectively.

Fig. 10 B is the cumulative pore volume for 3-D ACNs/GCDs sample. The sample show a very high total cumulative pore volume of 0.57 m3 g−1, where the pore volume between 0.4 and 1.0 nm is 0.31 m3 g−1, the pore volume between 0.4 and 2.3 nm is 0.46 m3 g−1, and the pore volume between 5.0 and 216 nm is 0.11 m3 g−1 (2.3–5.0 nm no contribution in pore volume). On the other hand, the pore size distribution of the sample (see the inset in Fig. 10 B) confirms that the carbon material has a unique hierarchical porous structure of “micropore (<2 nm) + mesopore (2–50 nm) + macropore (>50 nm)”, with hierarchical range of 0.4–1.0 nm (0.75 nm at peak value), 1.0–3.0 nm (1.52 nm at peak value), 5.0–216 nm (no obvious peak value). It can be seen that most of the porosity is concentrated in 0.4–2.3 nm, showing a double-peak-value (0.75 and 1.52 nm) continuous microporous structure.

The test results of electrochemical capacitor performance for 3-D ACNs/GCDs sample under three electrode system are shown in Fig. 11 . Fig. 11 A is the current-density-mode CV curves under different current densities (50, 100, 200, 500, 1000 mV s−1). It can be seen that in the tested potential range (0.0–1.2 V), the CV curves of different current densities show good rectangularity, indicating that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode has good capacitance characteristics and excellent power performance. With the increase of current density, the current density and area of CV Curve also gradually increase, which is consistent with the results reported in most literatures [41–43]. Fig. 11 B is the specific-capacitance-mode CV curves under different current densities. Obviously, these curves have good rectangle shapes, which further shows that the electrode material has good capacitance characteristics. However, with the increase of current density, the capacitance and area of CV Curve decrease slightly, which may be due to the lag of electrolyte diffusion and the inability of ion transfer rate to keep up at a faster scanning rate [44].

Fig. 11 C shows the CDC curve under different current densities (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 A g−1). It is very clear that these curves have good linear characteristics and isosceles triangle shape, which also proves that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode material has good capacitance characteristics [45]. Fig. 11 D shows the ratio capacitance curve, that is, the specific capacitance relationship under different current densities. Under the current density (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 A g−1), the specific capacitance distribution is 202.9, 169.7, 156.3, 148.3, 144.2 F g−1, and the capacitance retention is still 71% from 1 to 5 F g−1, which further shows that the three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets modified by graphitized carbon dots has excellent power performance [46]. The above specific capacitance (C
S ) is usually evaluated by CDC technology according to the following equation [47,48]:
(6)
Cs =
IΔt
m × ΔV
where I, Δt, ΔV and m are discharge current (A), discharge time (s), potential difference (V) and electrode mass (g), respectively.

Fig. 11 E and F demonstrate the EIS Nyquist plots of 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode in a 1 M KOH electrolyte (the inset in Fig. 11 E is an electrical equivalent circuit) and an enlarged view of its high-frequency part. The electrode shows typical low impedance and good capacitance characteristics, which presents an extremely small semicircle at high frequency and a highly vertical line at low frequency. The starting point of a semicircle represents the equivalent series resistance (Rs), including intrinsic resistance of active material, ion resistance of electrolyte and contact resistance of current collector. The diameter of semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct) in the electrode reaction process. The Rs and Rct of this electrode are very small, 3.77 Ω and 0.51 Ω, respectively, indicating that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode has high conductivity and fast charge transfer kinetics.

Fig. 12 shows the CDC curves of two electrode “button-like” supercapacitor of 3-D ACNs/GCDs. From the specific-capacitance-mode CV curves in Fig. 12 A, it is obvious that all curves have a good rectangular shape at different scanning rates (50, 100, 200, 500, 1000 mV s−1). Even at a higher scanning rate of 1000 mV s−1, the rectangularity remains good, which means that the electrode material has excellent capacitive performance and power characteristics under the practical conditions. This result can be attributed to the special structural advantages of “three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets” and “graphitized carbon dots”. The open three-dimensional structure is conducive to electrolyte penetration and charge transfer, and a large number of micropores and an appropriate amount of mesopores provide a very large electrochemical active area for the electrode [27–30]. In addition, graphitized carbon dots can greatly improve the electrical conductivity of activated carbon materials. Therefore, these structural advantages can make the 3-D ACNs/GCDs still has extremely fast dynamic characteristics and good capacitance performance even at high scanning rate, though the material is designed into 150-μm-thick disc electrode in “button-like” supercapacitor.

Fig. 12 B shows the CDC curves of “button-like” supercapacitor under different current densities (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 A g−1). It can be seen that there is a typical triangular symmetrical distribution in the potential range of 0–1.2 V, which shows the good capacitance characteristics of the supercapacitor. Based on the total weight of the two electrodes, the specific capacitance of supercapacitor can be calculated. Under different current densities (1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 A g−1), the total specific capacitance is 47.3, 38.1, 33.5, 31.2 and 29.9 F g−1, respectively. In particular, based on the series structure principle of two electrode symmetrical supercapacitor, the specific capacitance of a single disk electrode under corresponding current density can be converted to 189.6, 152.7, 134.3, 125.1 and 119.9 F g−1, respectively (see Fig. 12 C). It can be seen that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs material still has high power storage capacity and rate performance in the actual environment of supercapacitor applications. The specific capacitance conversion formula of a single disc electrode is as follows [49,50]:
(7)
C T
=
IΔt ΔV
,
1
C T
=
1
m 1
C s
+
1
m 2
C s
where C T is the total capacitance (F) of the two-electrode supercapacitor, and I is the current (a), ΔT is the discharge time (s), ΔV discharge voltage difference (V), C
S is the specific capacitance (F g−1) of a single disk electrode, and m 1 and m 2 are distributed as the mass of two electrodes.

The energy density (E) and power density (P) based on the two disk electrodes and single disk electrode are further calculated by Eqs. (8) and (9), respectively [11,51]. The Ragone plots (i.e., energy density as a function of power density) are showed in Fig. 12 D, where the fuel cells, secondary batteries and conventional capacitors are also compared with supercapacitors.
(8)
E =
1 2
Cs × Δ
V 2
×
1 3.6
(9)
P = E / Δt = 3600
where E is the energy density (W h kg−1), C
S is specific capacitance (F g−1), ΔV the discharge potential (V), P is the power density (W kg−1) and Δt is the discharging time (s). For the single disk electrode, the E of 3-D ACNs/GCDs electrode is 37.9 Wh kg−1 at a P of 600 W kg−1, which can maintain 24.0 Wh kg−1 when the P reaches 3000 W kg−1. For the two disk electrodes, the E of electrodes are 9.5 Wh kg−1 and 6.0 Wh kg−1, at power densities of 600 W kg−1 and 3000 W kg−1, respectively. Compared with other energy storage systems, the 3-D ACNs/GCDs//3-D ACNs/GCDs symmetric two-electrode supercapacitors possess a relatively high energy density at a very high power density.

Moreover, the EIS Nyquist plots of two-electrode supercapacitors with 150-μm-thick electrodes also display the perfect capacitance characteristics with a small semicircle (the Rs and Rct of this two-electrode device are very small, 2.68 Ω and 4.57 Ω) and a near-vertical line (see Fig. 12 E), manifesting ideal capacitive behavior and of the symmetric supercapacitors. The three-dimensional network nanosheet structure, unique graphitized carbon dot structure and abundant nanopore structure give the material good ion diffusion ability and excellent electronic conductivity. Therefore, the two-electrode supercapacitors demonstrated satisfactory electrochemical performances and good kinetic characteristics.

Since the cycle life is a crucial parameter for the supercapacitor, we further test the charge discharge cycle of the assembled “button-like” supercapacitor (6000 cycles at 1 A g−1 current density), and the results are shown in Fig. 12 F. It can be seen from the figure that after 2000 (or 6000) cycles, the total specific capacity of the capacitor decreased from 47.3 F g−1 to 44.1 F g−1 (or 41.5 F g−1) (the capacitance retention rate was as high as 93.2% (or 87.7%)). The specific capacitance converted into a single disk electrode is 189.6 F g−1 and 176.8 F g−1 (or 166.2 F g−1) before and after 2000 (or 6000) cycling, respectively. It can be seen that the 3-D ACNs/GCDs material has a high practical application prospect based on excellent electrochemical stability.

Numerous studies have been reported on carbon-based materials with different dimensions (such as 0-D, 1-D, 2-D carbon brick built 3-D nanostructures) for high-performance supercapacitors due to their unique dimensional and structural advantages [51]. Hereon, the new-type 3-D composite carbon electrode composed of hybrid-dimensional carbon materials (i.e., 0-D GCDs and 2-D ACNs hybrid 3-D nanostructures) is designed, which demonstrated satisfactory electrochemical performances in 3-electrode and 2-electrode supercapacitors when compared with other 3-D carbon electrode materials from recent literatures (see Table 1 for details) [25,26,52–57]. For these 3-D carbon materials, their different 3-D morphology (3-D nanosheets [25,26,53,57], 3-D nanocages [52], 3-D nanofibers [54], 3-D micro-particles [55], and 3-D micro-balls [56]), diversified crystalline or amorphous carbon structures (graphene, graphitized carbon, porous carbon, and activated carbon), varying specific surface area (300–2000 m2 g−1), and adjustable surface functional groups [54–57], resulted in the distinguishing specific capacitances (90–450 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) in 3-electrode supercapacitors. The specific capacitance of our 3-D ACNs/GCDs material is on an above average level (203 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in 3-electrode system), which might be further improved by increasing the specific surface area [26], proceeding surface modification (such as N-doping modification [6,15,56–58]), or increasing the electrolyte concentration [54–57]. It is worth mentioning that in the two-electrode system, our 3-D ACNs/GCDs material shows particularly satisfactory specific capacitance and desirable cycling stability when compared with other 3-D carbon materials. The above comparative analysis results demonstrate that the present 3-D ACNs/GCDs product has great room for capacitance improvement and is of clear application prospects in practical two-electrode supercapacitors.

In this paper, a convenient “buried heat treatment” technology based on muffle furnace is proposed to prepare “three-dimensional active carbon nanosheets/graphitized carbon dots (3-D ACNs/GCDs)” material by one-step KOH pyrolysis strategy. The KOH is used as activator (contributing a specific surface area of 1328 m2 g−1), 3-D structure template precursor (generating K2CO3 as template for 3-D carbon nanosheets), and a catalyst precursor (generating K2O as catalyst for graphitized carbon dots). As a promising supercapacitor electrode, the 3-D ACNs/GCDs shows relatively high specific capacitance of 202.9 F g−1 and 189.6 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 in 1 mol L−1 KOH aqueous solution, under the three-electrode system and “button-type” supercapacitor, respectively. This electrode material also shows high stability in actual capacitor environment where the capacitance retention rate is as high as 93.2% (or 87.7%) after 2000 (or 6000) cycles. These results generally indicated that the KOH-assist pyrolysis is an attractive strategy to design electrode material with satisfactory capacitive performances and a desirable durability for supercapacitors in actual applications.
Zesheng Li: Conceptualization; experimental design and data collection; data interpretation; writing-manuscript draft.
Hongqiang Wang: Conceptualization; experimental design and funding acquisition.
Bolin Li: experimental design; data collection; writing-manuscript draft.
Mei Yu: data interpretation.
Qingyu Li: writing-review & editing.
Changlin Yu: writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
